title: Consistent Rest & Recovery

content.question: Are you treating rest as earned luxury, or non-negotiable input?
sop: Estimate your current stress load and recovery time honestly (see calculation, page 5)
Schedule recovery blocks on the calendar the same way you'd schedule a client meeting
Pick one hard boundary, no after-hours email, one full off day, and hold it
Protect at least one full recovery cycle (a night's sleep, a weekend) from being the first thing cut when busy
Review monthly whether stress and recovery are actually in proportion, or drifting apart
content.explainer: Performance isn't just a function of how much stress you can handle, it's a function of stress relative to the recovery you've actually built in, and those two have to move together. Recovery that only happens after you've already broken down isn't the same system as recovery that's scheduled before you need it; only the second one actually protects output.
questions: Are you treating rest as earned luxury, or non-negotiable input?
When was the last time you scheduled recovery in advance, instead of collapsing into it after you'd already run out?
If your calendar is a fair record of your priorities, how much of it is actually recovery, not just the absence of meetings?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Stress 8, Recovery 3. Recovery ÷ Stress = 3 ÷ 8 = 0.38 — recovery isn't keeping up, so performance degrades even at high effort. Bring Recovery to 7: 7 ÷ 8 = 0.88, performance holds. Higher (recovery relative to stress) means sustained performance; a low ratio means you're borrowing against tomorrow.
content.closing_statement: Unscheduled recovery is the first thing that gets cut.

caption:
Principle Nº 217: Consistent Rest & Recovery

Schedule recovery like work. Performance isn't just a function of how much stress you can handle, it's a function of stress relative to the recovery you've actually built in, and those two have to move together.

Ask yourself: Are you treating rest as earned luxury, or non-negotiable input?
